rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, January 12, 2017, line 8. Motion To Strike Cross Complaint denied. The motion is moot in light of the court's ruling sustaining Old Republic's demurrer to the cross-complaint. = (501/REQ)

rulings.Case Title: OLD REPUBLIC NATIONAL TITLE INSURANCE COMPANY VS. CARJO PROPERTIES, LLC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, January 12, 2017, line 8. Cross Defendant's Demurrer To Cross Complaint sustained in its entirety without leave to amend. As to the third and fourth causes of action for breach of fiduciary duty and breach of contract, the cross-complaint fails to state a cause of action because Old Republic did not have a duty by law or under the Purchase and Sale Agreement and Escrow Instructions to identify and disclose all easements on the subject property. A title insurer "who has not undertaken to perform as an abstractor owes not duty to disclose . . . clouds on title." (Siegel v. Fidelity Nat'l. Title Ins. Co. (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 1181, 1189-1190.) Here, the cross-complaint does not allege, nor does it appear that it can allege, that Old Republic was to provide an abstract of title. While escrow agents have a duty to strictly comply with instructions from the parties, the agreement relied on in the cross-complaint merely required Old Republic to provide a commitment preliminary to issuance of a title insurance policy and copies of any referenced documents and easements to the buyer for its review. Nothing in the agreement required Old Republic to undertake an abstract of title, and consequently no law or agreement imposed a duty on Old Republic to discover and disclose all easements on the subject property to cross-complainants Carjo Properties LLC, Norman Bouton, and Joy Brorsen. As to the fifth cause of action for Unfair Competition, the claim fails as a matter of law. The Carjo defendants' claim is premised on the argument that Old Republic had a duty to identify and disclose all easements on the subject property, which it then shifted over to the seller. Since Old Republic had no such duty to breach or shift, the cross-complaint does not allege any wrongful business practice. As to the sixth and seventh causes of action for indemnity, the claims fail because they are premised the argument that Old Republic's had duty to disclose the 1963 light and air easement, but Old Republic has no such duty by law or under the agreement. = (501/REQ)

rulings.Case Title: ETHAN CHAN VS. HOWARD J. LEWIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer To Pltf'S Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, January 12, 2017, line 9. Demurrer To Plaintiff's Second Amended Complaint overruled in part and sustained in part. Mr. Lewis' demurrer is: a) overruled as to the first cause of action for negligent representation alleged against Mr. Lewis as trustee of the Jennie V. Maybon Trust, and b) sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for negligent misrepresentation alleged against Mr. Lewis in his individual capacity, as to the sixth cause of action for negligent nondisclosure alleged against Mr. Lewis both individually and as trustee, as to the seventh cause of action for negligence of owner/builder alleged against Mr. Lewis both individually and as trustee, and as to the eighth cause of action for negligence per se of owner/builder alleged against Mr. Lewis both individually and as trustee. Apart from the court's October 18, 2016 order on Mr. Lewis' demurrer to the first amended complaint and the propriety of the Chans' amendment adding three additional causes of action, the Chans' opposition to this motion is treated as a request for leave to amend. As to the first cause of action, the Chans adequately allege a claim for negligent misrepresentation against Mr. Lewis in his capacity as trustee of the Maybon Trust. The complaint indicates Defendant RE/MAX was acting as an agent of Mr. Lewis as trustee when advertising the property. Any alleged misrepresentations are attributable to Mr. Lewis as principal of its agent RE/MAX. As to the first cause of action alleged against Mr. Lewis individually, there are no facts alleging Mr. Lewis made misrepresentations in his individual capacity and it is not reasonably possible this defect can be cured by amendment. The sixth cause of action does not allege a cognizable negligent nondisclosure claim because Mr. Lewis was under no duty to disclose facts within the reach of the diligent attention and observation of the buyer. The court in Newhall Land and Farming Co. v. Superior Court (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 334, 349, stated "where the seller knows of facts materially affecting the value or desirability of the property¿and also knows that such facts are not known to, or within the reach of the diligent attention and observation of the buyer, the seller is under a duty to disclose them to the buyer." No such duty to disclose existed here because the Chans had access to permits as public records and were reasonably able to discover the alleged deficient work was not done by licensed contractors. Assuming without deciding Labor Code 2750.5 is inapplicable here, the seventh cause of action fails to allege a cognizable negligence claim because property sold "as-is" relieves the seller of all liability for defective conditions other than liability based on fraud, misrepresentation, or nondisclosure. (Shapiro v. Hu (1986) 188 Cal.App.3d 324, 332.) The eighth cause of action fails to allege a cognizable negligence per se claim and the Chans do not oppose the demurrer as to this cause of action. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the sixth, seventh, and eighth causes of action alleged against Mr. Lewis both individually and in his capacity as trustee because there is no reasonable possibility any defect can be cured by amendment. = (501/REQ)

rulings.Case Title: ANA MACDONALD VS. DUKE PARTNERS, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Restoring Hearing On Fair Rental Valuation Ot Calendar
rulings.Rulings: Real Property/Housing Court Law and Motion Calendar for Thursday, January 12, 2017, line 5. Restoring Hearing On Fair Rental Valuation to Calendar The motion to consolidate is GRANTED per Martin-Bragg v. Moore (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 367. Pursuant to Martin-Bragg Ana Macdonald shall post with the Court "fair compensation to [Duke Partners] for any delay in acquiring possession," which in an unlawful detainer is fair rental value of the premises. The fair rental value of the premises asserted in the operative Second Amended Complaint is $150/day. Duke Partners present uncontroverted evidence that fair maker rental value of the premises is $4,700. Macdonald assertion that the compensation to Duke Partners must be measured based on a contract between Macdonald and Nationstar is not supported by any authority. Therefore, the amount of monthly posting is set at $4,500. Macdonald shall post $16,500 with the Court no later than January 20, 2017 and then post $4,500 on or before the 5th day of each month starting February 2017 for the pendency of the litigation or further order of the Court. = (501/REQ)
